interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Southern Oral History Program, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill / Library of Congress

main_summary.summary:
Richard Barry Sobol recounts his transition from a Washington, D.C. corporate law associate at Arnold, Fortas & Porter to a permanent civil rights lawyer in New Orleans, Louisiana, during the mid-to-late 1960s. Born in New York City and educated at the Bronx High School of Science, Union College, and Columbia Law School, Sobol clerked for Judge Paul R. Hays on the United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit, briefly worked at the Federal Trade Commission, and joined Arnold, Fortas & Porter in spring 1963. In summer 1965, he was assigned by the Lawyers Constitutional Defense Committee (LCDC), headed by Henry Schwarzschild, as a three-week volunteer in New Orleans, where he obtained a swift reversal from the Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals in a school desegregation case originally dismissed by Judge E. Gordon West in Baton Rouge. Convinced that the South needed permanent rather than rotating volunteer lawyers, Sobol returned in summer 1966 as LCDC's first permanent New Orleans staff attorney, working in association with Collins, Douglas, and Elie -- the principal Black law firm representing the Congress of Racial Equality (CORE) in Louisiana, whose partners were Robert F. Collins, Nils Douglas, and Lolis Elie.

Much of Sobol's work centered on Bogalusa, Louisiana, where the Bogalusa Voters League -- led by A.Z. Young as president, Robert (Bob) Hicks as vice president, and Gayle Jenkins as secretary -- waged sustained pressure on the segregated paper-mill town. Sobol prepared and litigated employment discrimination cases against Crown Zellerbach under the recently enacted Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, attacking the company's racially segregated lines of progression, its job-seniority system, and its use of the Wonderlic test as a screening device. The Crown Zellerbach case, tried before federal District Judge Frederick J.R. Heebe with co-counsel George Cooper, produced a preliminary injunction; Sobol and Cooper subsequently co-authored an influential Harvard Law Review article (published in 1969) advocating plant-wide seniority and elimination of lines of progression as remedies for the discriminatory effects of facially neutral seniority systems. The article was widely cited and influential in lower-court rulings until the Supreme Court's 1976/77 Teamsters decision narrowed its theory.

Sobol also handled the criminal defense of Gary Duncan, a young Black man in Plaquemines Parish charged with battery after touching a white teenager's elbow during an attempted altercation between Black and white students near a recently desegregated school. After Duncan's conviction, Sobol was himself arrested by Leander Perez and a sheriff at the courthouse in Plaquemines Parish on charges of practicing law without a license; the resulting federal case, tried before a three-judge court including Judges Fred J. Cassibry, Frederick J.R. Heebe, and Robert A. Ainsworth Jr., resulted in an injunction protecting out-of-state civil rights lawyers practicing in association with local counsel. The Duncan criminal appeal reached the U.S. Supreme Court, which in Duncan v. Louisiana ruled that the Fourteenth Amendment requires jury trials in state criminal proceedings for non-petty offenses. Sobol's other work in Louisiana included voting-rights litigation in Madison Parish (the Zelma Wyche police-chief election and the Harrison Brown school-board election, both heard before a federal judge in Monroe), a fair-housing case under Hicks v. Weaver involving the placement of public housing in Bogalusa, welfare-fraud defense in New Orleans federal court that produced United States v. McDaniels (a District Court ruling restricting the racial use of peremptory challenges, anticipating Batson v. Kentucky by roughly two decades), and a case in Lafayette protecting the Southern Consumers Cooperative -- a Black sweet-potato farmers' cooperative organized with CORE worker John Zippert and Catholic priest Father Albert J. McKnight -- from district-attorney harassment.

Sobol left LCDC employment in December 1968, taught for one semester at the University of Michigan Law School, then joined Marian Wright Edelman's Washington Research Project (the predecessor to the Children's Defense Fund), returned to Louisiana from 1971 to 1974 (during which he and his wife Anne Sobol worked with Lolis Elie on the welfare-fraud cases), and eventually opened a private firm in Washington, D.C. with Mike Trister. He reflects that the experience transformed him irrevocably -- making a return to corporate antitrust work impossible -- and observes that the same contagion changed many of the short-term volunteer lawyers and law students who came through Louisiana.
key_themes: School Desegregation and Freedom of Choice
Right to Counsel and Out-of-State Civil Rights Lawyers
Bogalusa Movement and Local Black Leadership
Voting Rights Enforcement in Rural Louisiana
Personal Transformation of a Civil Rights Lawyer
main_summary.historical_significance: Sobol's account documents the day-to-day mechanics of federal civil rights litigation in Louisiana from 1965 through the mid-1970s, including the legal architecture that translated the Civil Rights Act of 1964, the Voting Rights Act of 1965, and the federal Fair Housing law into operational remedies. His testimony illuminates a less-narrated dimension of the movement -- the role of permanent LCDC staff lawyers working in association with local Black firms like Collins, Douglas, and Elie -- and provides firsthand provenance for two enduring constitutional precedents (Duncan v. Louisiana on jury trial in state criminal cases, and the McDaniels peremptory-challenge ruling that anticipated Batson v. Kentucky) as well as the Harvard Law Review seniority-systems theory that shaped lower-court Title VII jurisprudence between 1969 and 1976. The interview also offers a granular insider portrait of the Bogalusa Voters League leadership -- A.Z. Young, Robert Hicks, and Gayle Jenkins -- and a notably skeptical perspective on the historiography of the Deacons for Defense and Justice.

errors: Interview date is not stated on the transcript and is left as 'unknown' in metadata; the audit could not confirm a precise calendar date from the SRT text alone.

The summary uses 'Fred J. Cassibry' as the lead trial judge for the three-judge court because the transcript names 'Judge Cassibry' (segments 843, 863, 939) without first name confirmation; the spelling and initials are inferred from convention and may need verification.

Summary states the Crown Zellerbach trial produced a 'preliminary injunction' followed by a written opinion 'three years later,' tracking Sobol's phrasing at segments 537-539; the precise interval was given by the witness from memory and is potentially imprecise.

Summary states the Sobol-Cooper Harvard Law Review article was published in 1969; transcript references '69' in interviewer question (segment 732) but Sobol's own account does not name the year, so the year is interviewer-supplied.
improvements: Confirm the interview date from external metadata (catalog record or accompanying documentation) rather than leaving as 'unknown.'
Cross-check the spelling/full name of Judge Fred J. Cassibry against Eastern District of Louisiana records.
Verify the citation 'Hicks v. Weaver, 302 F. Supp. 619' against Westlaw; transcript voices the case name and citation but the audio/correction overlay may have rendering errors.
Confirm whether the Sobol-Cooper article is the 1969 piece commonly cited (82 Harv. L. Rev. 1598) or a different Harvard Law Review publication.

chapters.title: Early Life, Law School, and Arnold, Fortas & Porter
chapters.summary: Sobol describes his New York City upbringing on Manhattan's west side -- his father a lawyer, his mother a high school mathematics teacher. He attended the Bronx High School of Science, Union College, and returned to New York for Columbia Law School. He recalls that he accepted from an early age that he would become a lawyer because his father was one. As a law student, he wrote a published piece on a Supreme Court Alabama gerrymander decision but otherwise was focused on coursework. After law school he clerked for Judge Paul R. Hays on the United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit, briefly served as a special assistant to a commissioner at the Federal Trade Commission, and joined Arnold, Fortas & Porter as an associate in spring 1963. He notes he held 'illusions' that the firm would be doing more right-to-counsel and McCarthy Committee defense work in the mold of Abe Fortas's Supreme Court case, but found the actual practice 'quite different.'
related_events: United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit

improvements: Could note the specific year of the gerrymander piece if the article can be identified externally.

chapters.title: Volunteering with the Lawyers Constitutional Defense Committee, Summer 1965
chapters.summary: In spring 1964, two colleagues at Arnold, Fortas & Porter told Sobol about the Lawyers Constitutional Defense Committee (LCDC), which sent attorneys south during the summer to handle civil rights arrests. Sobol volunteered in 1964 but was not selected because he was not admitted in any of the Deep South states. Over the following winter he met LCDC director Henry Schwarzschild, complained about not being selected, and was promised a place in summer 1965. The assignment letter named New Orleans -- a 'big disappointment' to Sobol, who had hoped for Selma or Jackson. He arrived on August 1, 1965, for a three-week assignment and describes the pace as 'breakneck' compared to Washington practice. On his first day he was sent to Baton Rouge to meet Murphy Bell, took the school desegregation papers from clients in St. Francisville (West Feliciana Parish), filed the case before Judge E. Gordon West (a 'well-known racist judge' who dismissed it the next day), and within a week obtained a summary reversal from the Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals. After three weeks Sobol concluded that the South needed permanent rather than rotating volunteer lawyers.

improvements: Confirm the exact date (Sobol says 'first of August 1965').

chapters.title: Collins, Douglas, and Elie and the Bogalusa Lawyers
chapters.summary: Sobol describes his collaboration during summer 1965 with Collins, Douglas, and Elie -- the firm of Robert F. Collins (who would later become 'the first black federal judge in the South'), Nils Douglas, and Lolis Elie. The firm's office at 2200 Dryad Street housed CORE on the ground floor and the law firm on the second floor. Sobol observes that the firm did paid 'poor black neighborhood' work -- 'automobile accidents, bankruptcies, land law-attentant issues' -- as well as the civil rights work for CORE, and that the firm's daily late-afternoon gathering in Collins's office (with a bottle of scotch) was 'a nice contrast' to Arnold, Fortas & Porter. He describes meeting the Bogalusa leaders -- Bob Hicks, Gayle Jenkins, and A.Z. Young -- and recounts his first Bogalusa appearance with Nils Douglas before a hostile local judge who slammed his crutch on the table after being told Sobol came from Abe Fortas's firm. Sobol returned as permanent LCDC staff in late July 1966 and inherited 'four inches thick' of case files compiled by volunteer Jerry Gutman.

improvements: Could verify the address '2200 Dryad Street' externally.

chapters.title: The Bogalusa Voters League and Its Leadership
chapters.summary: Sobol describes Bogalusa as 'hot and smelly because of the paper mill,' with nightly meetings at a union hall near Robert Hicks's house. He recounts the daily Main Street protest marches against discrimination by store owners, and notes that Title VII of the Civil Rights Act had taken effect four weeks before his arrival, focusing attention on Crown Zellerbach -- the major employer with four plants making paper products, boxes, and wrapping paper. Sobol attributes the Bogalusa movement's intensity to three factors: the unbending segregationist line of the town's white power structure ('there wasn't much leeway'), the presence of unionized paper-mill workers experienced in organization, and the support from a San Francisco-based group (because Crown Zellerbach was a San Francisco company) that sent money and hosted Bogalusa leaders. He describes Gayle Jenkins as the organizational spark who recruited A.Z. Young as president because she felt a man should publicly lead it, and notes that A.Z. Young, Bob Hicks (Jenkins's cousin), and Hicks's wife played complementary roles. The Hicks home on what is now Robert Hicks Avenue became the gathering point for civil rights visitors, including James Farmer and Lincoln Lynch (CORE's second-in-command). Sobol notes that the (SNCC) Freedom Singers and the Free Southern Theater performed at the union hall.

improvements: Confirm whether the 'Free Southern Theater' is referenced correctly (Sobol corrects himself mid-sentence).

chapters.title: Crown Zellerbach Title VII Litigation and Federal Judges
chapters.summary: Sobol explains how the Crown Zellerbach Title VII case proceeded to trial in summer 1967 before federal District Judge Frederick J.R. Heebe, consolidated with a government case (United States v. Local 1). Sobol attributes his methodical use of discovery -- interrogatories, document production, depositions, motions to compel -- to his antitrust background at Arnold, Fortas & Porter, and notes that LCDC paid for deposition transcripts. He credits Judge Heebe (newly appointed, with nothing in his background suggesting sympathy to civil rights cases) and the Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals more broadly for being responsive when 'you were entitled to overnight' relief. He recalls that George Cooper handled the testing-discrimination side and Sobol the seniority side, and that Quarles v. Philip Morris (a Virginia case before Judge Butzner, with Gabrielle Kirk McDonald representing the plaintiffs) provided precedent. He also describes consultation with Civil Rights Division attorneys at the Department of Justice -- particularly Robert Moore and David Rose -- on the discriminatory effects of facially neutral seniority systems.
related_events: Department of Justice Civil Rights Division
Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964

errors: Sobol mentions 'Judge Fosson' (sic) as having decided Quarles; this is almost certainly Judge John D. Butzner Jr., but the transcript itself gives an unclear name -- summary uses the transcript spelling as 'Butzner' inferred from context only if verified, otherwise marks as ambiguous.
improvements: Verify the judge's name in Quarles v. Philip Morris.

chapters.title: School Desegregation and Mill Conditions in Bogalusa
chapters.summary: Sobol describes how Southern school desegregation orders had produced only the most token results -- a 'freedom of choice' regime in which two or three Black children would attend the previously all-white school while everyone else remained in segregated facilities. He recalls visiting Bogalusa's Black schools after a desegregation order had issued and finding them 'blatantly run down' compared with the white schools, with teachers paid less and supplies withheld. The office's efforts to dismantle freedom-of-choice -- arguing for unitary school districts -- produced what he believes was the first such order in the South from Judge Frederick J.R. Heebe in Washington Parish, and was later achieved 'on a whole regional basis' by the Supreme Court (a reference to the 1969 Alexander v. Holmes 'no more deliberate speed' ruling). With respect to Crown Zellerbach, Sobol describes touring the plants by court order and observing that most jobs were simple repetitive work that any 'reasonably intelligent person' could learn in two or three days, undercutting the company's defense that Black workers needed years of preparation. The highest-paying 'black' job under the pre-Title VII contract was picking finished product off the assembly line; the lowest 'white' job started immediately above it. He notes that the bag plants employed white women but no Black workers, producing an intersecting race-and-sex discrimination issue.
related_events: Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964

errors: Reference to the Supreme Court regional desegregation ruling is paraphrased by Sobol but not named in the transcript; summary should not name Alexander v. Holmes explicitly without a transcript anchor.
improvements: Remove or hedge the Alexander v. Holmes attribution since the transcript itself only says 'the Supreme Court' decided this 'on a whole regional basis.'

chapters.title: The Deacons, the Klan, and the Question of Violence
chapters.summary: Sobol offers what he calls an 'unorthodox' perspective on the Deacons for Defense and Justice: 'In the time I was living here and working on problems in Bogalusa, I never heard about the Deacons.' He recalls that friends of the Hicks family climbed on the roof with rifles in response to threats against Hicks's household, but says that at the time he understood this as friends acting individually rather than as members of a named organization. He says historians have made the Deacons story more prominent than his recollection supports, and that Lolis Elie agrees with his impression. Sobol acknowledges that there was Klan violence -- including occasions where shots were fired at marches and someone was hurt -- but says these events did not happen when he was around. He references Hicks v. Knight, a case that had concluded the day before he arrived as a 1965 volunteer, in which Judge Herbert W. Christenberry enjoined every Bogalusa police officer over the lack of protection of marches. He describes the march to Baton Rouge, where National Guardsmen formed a barrier between marchers and observers through Livingston Parish (then believed to be Klan headquarters); he saw glass soda bottles thrown over the police line but no firearms.
related_events: March from Bogalusa to Baton Rouge

improvements: Flag that this chapter is intentionally framed as Sobol's perspective, which is at variance with the standard historiography of the Bogalusa Deacons chapter.

chapters.title: The Crown Zellerbach Trial: Testing and Seniority
chapters.summary: Sobol recounts the Crown Zellerbach trial in detail. George Cooper arrived with a trunk full of books -- prior writings by the defense's own expert witnesses contradicting their trial testimony -- and used them in cross-examination to 'make them look like completed heets.' On the seniority issue, Sobol and Cooper used demonstrative charts to show Judge Frederick J.R. Heebe the impact of the existing system versus a remedy that disregarded pre-Title VII allocations. The trial ran approximately eight days, with Bob Hicks sitting next to Sobol the entire time and roughly thirty Crown Zellerbach workers in attendance. Sobol explains the Wonderlic test -- a roughly 20-30 question short-answer test measuring vocabulary, geography, and spatial relations that had never been used at Crown Zellerbach before the Civil Rights Act and was suddenly imposed as a hiring requirement, disqualifying about eight in ten Black applicants. He cites the canonical 'What is RSVP me?' question as an example of cultural bias. On the seniority side, Sobol explains how Crown Zellerbach had merged previously segregated job lines by pay rate -- placing all formerly Black jobs at the bottom -- and how 'job seniority' meant only time in the next-lower job mattered, not time at the plant. The lawyers argued for plant-wide seniority and elimination of lines of progression so that a senior Black employee could bid into any open position he was qualified to perform.
related_events: Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964

chapters.title: The Harvard Law Review Article and Union Backlash
chapters.summary: Sobol describes the seniority-systems theory developed in his Harvard Law Review article, co-authored with George Cooper. The theory was that a facially neutral seniority system was 'discriminatory' under Title VII because of its disparate impact on Black employees historically excluded from white jobs, regardless of intent. Sobol notes that he and Cooper were already friends in Washington (Cooper at Covington & Burling, Sobol at Arnold, Fortas & Porter) and split the writing in half, editing each other's drafts. The Harvard Law Review extensively rearranged the manuscript before publication. Sobol recalls Cooper telling him the piece became, by one organization's count, the most cited Law Review article in many years. The theory shaped lower-court Title VII decisions and Department of Labor and Office of Federal Contract Compliance practice from roughly 1967/68 through 1975/76, until the Supreme Court ruled against the theory in (Sobol believes) United States v. Teamsters in 1975 or 1976. Sobol describes a personal cost: when he later represented unions in private practice, the AFL-CIO leadership 'still held it against' him for the article, treating union-side advocacy as incompatible with his earlier scholarship.
related_events: Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964

errors: Sobol gives the Supreme Court Teamsters date as '1976, maybe 75' from memory; summary preserves this uncertainty without external verification.
improvements: Confirm the actual Teamsters citation (likely Teamsters v. United States, 431 U.S. 324 (1977)).

chapters.title: Gary Duncan, Leander Perez, and Duncan v. Louisiana
chapters.summary: In early 1967, Gary Duncan (a 19-year-old Black man) and his mother came to Sobol's office after being referred by the Department of Justice Civil Rights Division. Duncan had touched the elbow of a white teenager near a recently desegregated Plaquemines Parish school while trying to defuse a confrontation involving two of his nephews; the father of one of the white boys reported it as a slap, and Duncan was arrested and charged with battery (punishable by two years). Sobol describes the parish as ruled by Leander Perez -- a 'well-known racist figure' who fought desegregation, espoused interposition and secession theories, and once threatened to put any civil rights activist 'on Prince Philip Island with the allocators.' Perez's son was the district attorney; the elder Perez appointed the judges. Judge Herbert W. Christenberry had nonetheless ordered desegregation in Plaquemines. Sobol's challenge to the absence of a jury (Louisiana then denied juries for offenses punishable by under two years) was denied; Duncan was convicted and sentenced to 60 days and a fine. While Sobol was at the courthouse arranging an appeal stay, Leander Perez and a sheriff arrested him for practicing law without a license. Don Juneau bailed him out the same afternoon. The resulting federal suit, before a three-judge court including Judges Fred J. Cassibry, Frederick J.R. Heebe, and Robert A. Ainsworth Jr., produced an injunction protecting out-of-state civil rights lawyers practicing in association with local counsel. Duncan v. Louisiana then reached the U.S. Supreme Court, which ruled that the Fourteenth Amendment requires jury trials in state criminal proceedings except for the most minor offenses. Louisiana retried Duncan with a jury, but a follow-on case (Duncan v. Perez, before Judge Cassibry) enjoined the prosecution as malicious. Sobol notes that Duncan went on to become 'an important leader' in the parish and head of a shrimper organization.
related_events: Department of Justice Civil Rights Division

errors: Transcript renders Perez's island threat as 'Prince Philip Island with the allocators' -- the canonical phrasing is 'Pencen Island with the alligators' or similar; summary preserves transcript verbatim but the underlying claim is partially corrupted by Whisper transcription.
improvements: If a higher-confidence rendering of Perez's quotation is available externally, prefer it; otherwise flag as Whisper-rendering uncertainty.

chapters.title: Madison Parish Voting Rights Cases
chapters.summary: After passage of the Voting Rights Act of 1965 and the substantial registration of Black voters in upstate Louisiana parishes, Sobol litigated two Madison Parish cases. In the first, Zelma Wyche -- head of the Madison Parish voters league -- ran for police chief and lost by 10-15 votes after the clerk of court collected roughly 200 absentee ballots from white nursing-home patients (and from no Black old-age homes). Heard by a judge named Dawkins in Monroe, the case produced a novel doctrine the judge called 'discrimination in fact' (no discriminatory intent but discriminatory effect), and the election was set aside; Wyche won the second election and was police chief for many years. In the second case, Harrison Brown, a Black teacher running for the school board, was disconnected from the Democratic Party 'rooster' lever on the ballot so that voters pulling the party-line lever did not vote for him. The same Monroe judge again ruled 'discrimination in fact' and ordered a new election, which Brown won.
related_events: Federal District Court in Monroe, Louisiana

improvements: Sobol's memory on which case the federal government joined is uncertain; preserved as such.

chapters.title: Hicks v. Weaver and Public Housing in Bogalusa
chapters.summary: Robert Hicks brought Sobol the plans for federal public-housing construction in Bogalusa, having noticed that the housing was being sited deep inside existing Black or white neighborhoods rather than in mixed areas. Sobol researched and concluded that the placement violated the non-discrimination provisions of a recently enacted civil rights/fair-housing law. The federal government (HUD), which had been allied with Sobol's office on most other cases, became the defendant here. Judge Cassibry found a private right of action and that the siting was discriminatory, enjoining the construction; the bulldozers were halted at the last minute. The reported decision is Hicks v. Weaver, 302 F. Supp. 619 (E.D. La. 1969). Sobol presents the case as an example of Hicks's creativity in 'spotting every aspect' of segregation, also citing the football-team and prom-related disputes from the school-desegregation litigation in which Judge Frederick J.R. Heebe was drawn into adjudicating details like coaching and player positions.
related_events: Department of Housing and Urban Development

improvements: Verify Hicks v. Weaver citation against Westlaw; transcript voices the citation but reader-confirmation is prudent.

chapters.title: Welfare Fraud Cases, U.S. v. McDaniels, and Career Path
chapters.summary: Anne Sobol (Sobol's then-wife) and Sobol, with Lolis Elie, represented a group of approximately twenty women being prosecuted in New Orleans federal court for welfare fraud. Of the roughly twenty women charged, only one (with a prior record) served three months in prison in West Virginia. The case advanced two important procedural issues: a challenge to the grand-jury composition's underrepresentation of poor people (using computer analysis of food-stamp tapes and voter rolls showing 30% of food-stamp recipients were registered to vote versus 78% of all others), and -- after the prosecutor in the first trial used peremptory challenges to remove every Black juror -- a successful argument before Judge Alvin B. Rubin that peremptory challenges could not be used on the basis of race. The resulting District Court ruling, United States v. McDaniels, anticipated by roughly twenty years the Supreme Court's adoption of the same principle in Batson v. Kentucky, 476 U.S. 79. Sobol describes his post-LCDC trajectory: he left Louisiana in August 1968, ended LCDC employment in December 1968, taught one semester at the University of Michigan Law School (Thursday-Friday schedule with Saturday-through-Wednesday return trips to Louisiana), joined Marian Wright Edelman's Washington Research Project (predecessor to the Children's Defense Fund) for two years, returned to Louisiana in 1971 (during which Anne Sobol joined him), and moved back to Washington in 1974 where he eventually opened a private firm with Mike Trister representing unions.

improvements: The 'Golf first' phrase in transcript segment 988 is unclear -- the case is likely Castaneda or another procedural ruling -- summary omits this reference rather than misattribute it.

chapters.title: Southern Consumers Cooperative and the Zippert Wedding
chapters.summary: Sobol describes a case arising near Opelousas and Lafayette, Louisiana, where many Black sweet-potato farmers had long been selling at low prices to a white wholesaler. With CORE worker John Zippert and Catholic priest Father Albert J. McKnight, local farmers formed a cooperative (the Southern Consumers Cooperative cooperative) to process, package, and sell the potatoes directly. The white power structure -- including several harmed wholesalers -- resented the cooperative; the district attorney called members before grand juries and one night had the police break in and seize the cooperative's files. Sobol sued in federal court in Lafayette to enjoin the harassment and recover the documents. The district attorney was unable to justify his actions before the federal judge and was enjoined. The cooperative still exists. After the case, Sobol befriended John Zippert, a white CORE worker who wanted to marry Carol Zippert, a young Black woman from the area. The local clerk of court refused to issue a marriage license because the marriage was interracial; Sobol filed a federal suit immediately after the Supreme Court's Loving v. Virginia decision and obtained an order requiring the clerk to issue the license. Sobol attended the wedding.

improvements: Confirm the cooperative's precise name and the relationship between Zippert and Carol Zippert (Sobol's account treats them as bride/groom).

chapters.title: Personal Transformation and Reflection
chapters.summary: Sobol reflects on the cumulative effect of the LCDC years. He revises his earlier dismissive comment about short-term volunteer lawyers, noting that even the briefest stints produced lasting change in those who served -- many of whom later led human-rights commissions in Pittsburgh, Cincinnati, and other cities -- and that law students who came down through 'law students civil committee for civil rights' similarly went into public-service or pro-bono work after their visits. Sobol explains that the cumulative impact of the legal work, the Bogalusa community, and the broader social context (including the Vietnam War, which he was discussing with anti-war activists) made it impossible for him to return to Arnold, Fortas & Porter and 'be slaughtered into some anti-trust case representing one big corporation against another.' He credits the union law clients he developed in private practice with making it financially possible to sustain a civil rights practice. He notes that as a young lawyer at LCDC he had argued before the U.S. Supreme Court -- an experience most large-firm associates never have in an entire career.
related_events: Law Students Civil Rights Research Council

interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Southern Oral History Program, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill / Library of Congress

evidence: Sobol opens with chronological background and consistently traces his arrival in New Orleans, his work patterns, and his post-LCDC career through 1974 (Segment 1, 00:00:00,000; Segment 1011, 01:43:55,000)

Interviewer Mosnier scaffolds the structure with chapter-like prompts -- 'Let me take you back at the bubble list' (Segment 541, 00:38:52,000); 'Let me turn your remember back to the very unusual episode in 1967' (Segment 810, 01:24:05,000) -- producing a near-thematic organization
story_structure_coherence.justification: The interview follows a chronological-then-thematic arc, opening with Sobol's biography, moving through his volunteer-then-permanent LCDC years, then through case-by-case discussion of Crown Zellerbach, Duncan, Madison Parish voting, Hicks v. Weaver, welfare cases, and the sweet-potato cooperative, before closing with reflection on personal transformation. Mosnier's prompts keep the structure organized; occasional backtracking (the second example on seniority is added mid-Harvard-Law-Review answer) is minor.

most_vivid_moment.quote: And this judge used a crutch, one crutch to walk, he took his crutch, and he slammed it on the table and he said, don't talk to me about the Supreme Court of the United States.

evidence: George Cooper's trunk of books used to cross-examine Crown Zellerbach experts (Segment 673-681, ~00:54:24)
The Bogalusa city-court judge slamming his crutch after being told Sobol came from Abe Fortas's firm (Segment 131, 00:16:34)
Sobol's arrest by Leander Perez and a sheriff in the Plaquemines Parish courthouse hallway (Segment 837, 01:26:15)
Wonderlic 'What is RSVP me?' question as illustration of cultural bias (Segment 708, 01:00:50)
vivid_details_examples.justification: Sobol provides numerous concrete scenes -- the crutch-slamming judge, the trunk of books, the arrest, the bulldozers halted at the last minute, the 'two-day hearing on a Friday and Saturday about the selection of the prom queen.' Names and specific case citations (Hicks v. Weaver, 302 F. Supp. 619; Batson v. Kentucky, 476 U.S. 79) anchor the testimony. The score is held below 9 only because several memories are explicitly framed as approximate ('1976, maybe 75').

most_authentic_moment.quote: I just got got into it, and it was doing it, trying to organize it, and some, and I worked alone, but I honestly did basically the civil rights work with, I was in association with the lawyers upstairs

emotional_range: disappointment with the legal profession's current state
vulnerability_elements: Acknowledges early 'illusions' about Arnold, Fortas & Porter
Names personal cost of the Harvard Law Review article in union-representation work
Concedes his contrarian view of the Deacons may simply reflect what he missed
emotional_authenticity.justification: Sobol is reflective and willing to flag where his memory is uncertain and where his perspective is contrarian (the Deacons), and he names the personal headwinds from the AFL-CIO leadership over his Harvard Law Review article. The interview is more analytical-professional than emotionally raw; Sobol does not dwell on fear, grief, or interior moral struggle even when describing his own arrest.

narrative_quality.overall_assessment: Narrative quality is strong: the structure is well-organized chronologically and thematically, vivid specific scenes anchor the major cases, and Sobol's voice is reflective and self-aware. The interview leans more analytical-professional than emotionally intimate, which is appropriate for an attorney recounting litigation but limits the emotional-authenticity score.

major_events: Crown Zellerbach Title VII trial before Judge Heebe, summer 1967 (Segment 431-540, ~00:31:58 onward)
Duncan v. Louisiana criminal defense and arrest by Leander Perez (Segment 812-867, ~01:16:50 onward)
Hicks v. Weaver public-housing siting case (Segment 921-951, ~01:34:53 onward)
Sobol-Cooper Harvard Law Review article on seniority systems, 1969 (Segment 732-786, ~01:06:00 onward)
Founding work of the Southern Consumers Cooperative (Segment 1053-1086, ~01:47:08 onward)

key_figures.name: Robert (Bob) Hicks
key_figures.relationship: Lead client in Bogalusa employment-discrimination, school-desegregation, and fair-housing cases; sat with Sobol throughout Crown Zellerbach trial

key_figures.name: A.Z. Young
key_figures.relationship: President of the Bogalusa Voters League; spoke at rallies and led marches Sobol participated in

key_figures.name: Gayle Jenkins
key_figures.relationship: Secretary of the Bogalusa Voters League; Sobol recorded interviews with her after his return to Louisiana in the 1990s

key_figures.name: Lolis Elie
key_figures.relationship: Law partner at Collins, Douglas, and Elie; lifelong friend

key_figures.name: Henry Schwarzschild
key_figures.relationship: Director of the Lawyers Constitutional Defense Committee who recruited Sobol

key_figures.name: George Cooper
key_figures.relationship: Co-counsel in Crown Zellerbach trial and co-author of the Harvard Law Review seniority article

key_figures.name: Leander Perez
key_figures.relationship: Antagonist who arrested Sobol in Plaquemines Parish

key_figures.name: Gary Duncan
key_figures.relationship: Client whose criminal defense became Duncan v. Louisiana

key_figures.name: Marian Wright Edelman
key_figures.relationship: Employed Sobol at the Washington Research Project (predecessor to Children's Defense Fund) after his LCDC years

organizational_involvement.role: Volunteer (summer 1965); permanent New Orleans staff attorney (summer 1966 - December 1968)
Associated counsel on CORE-affiliated civil rights litigation

organizational_involvement.organization: Washington Research Project / Children's Defense Fund
organizational_involvement.role: Staff attorney working on government civil rights enforcement

unique_firsthand_perspective.unique_access: As LCDC's first permanent New Orleans staff attorney, Sobol had direct attorney-client access to the Bogalusa Voters League leadership, the Black union locals at Crown Zellerbach, and federal Civil Rights Division lawyers (Robert Moore, David Rose); he was personally arrested by Leander Perez and personally argued before the U.S. Supreme Court in Duncan v. Louisiana.
unique_firsthand_perspective.justification: Sobol's vantage was that of a 'movement lawyer' embedded in the local legal community for several years, with personal participation in multiple cases that became foundational Title VII, jury-trial, and peremptory-challenge precedents. The score is held to 9 (not 10) because he was not a Movement leader in the political sense, was not at the most-famous events (Selma, March on Washington), and frames much of his most consequential work in terms of co-counsel collaboration rather than solo influence.

precision_examples: Crown Zellerbach trial 'eight days' duration with 'about 30 people' attending and Bob Hicks sitting next to Sobol the entire time (Segment 688-693, ~00:56:00)
Wonderlic test described as roughly 20-30 questions covering vocabulary, geography, and spatial relations (Segment 697-701, ~00:58:00)
Reported decisions cited by name and number -- Hicks v. Weaver, 302 F. Supp. 619 (Segment 951, 01:37:00); Batson v. Kentucky, 476 U.S. 79 (Segment 1001, 01:42:36)
Specific firm address: 'the office of Collins, Douglas, and Eli was on the second floor in the 2200 block of Dryad Street' (Segment 66-67, ~00:10:29)
Gary Duncan was 19 years old at the time of his arrest (Segment 812, 01:16:50)
verifiable_claims: Crown Zellerbach case consolidated with United States v. Local 1 (Segment 444-445, 00:32:40)
Duncan v. Louisiana ruling on Fourteenth Amendment jury-trial right (Segment 860, 01:27:51)
Hicks v. Knight injunction by Judge Herbert W. Christenberry against Bogalusa police (Segment 650-653, 00:51:03)
Harvard Law Review article co-authored with George Cooper (Segment 732-765, 01:06:00)
United States v. McDaniels peremptory-challenge ruling by Judge Alvin B. Rubin (Segment 997-999, 01:42:10)
specificity_of_details.justification: Sobol's account is replete with named cases, named judges, named clients, specific addresses, specific dollar/numeric figures, and verifiable institutional affiliations. Some dates are given as approximate by the witness's own admission. The score is held to 7 (not 8) because a few names and a few year-dates are hedged ('1976, maybe 75').

new_information: Detailed first-person account of how LCDC's permanent-staff model evolved out of the volunteer-lawyer program, including specific budgetary substitution arithmetic
Insider description of how Crown Zellerbach's merged-line seniority system worked numerically and how the remedy of plant-wide seniority + elimination of lines of progression was operationalized
Procedural account of the Sobol-Cooper Harvard Law Review article's drafting (split-in-half authorship, Harvard editing) and its 1967-76 zenith before the Teamsters retrenchment
Skeptical perspective on the Bogalusa Deacons for Defense and Justice from a contemporaneous lawyer for the Voters League
understudied_aspects: Daily mechanics of federal civil rights litigation in Louisiana parishes outside the well-covered Mississippi context
The role of southern federal judges (Heebe, Christenberry, Cassibry, Ainsworth) in operationalizing the 1964 Civil Rights Act
Title VII litigation as community-anchored advocacy (Bob Hicks sitting next to Sobol for eight trial days)
The economic-cooperative dimension of Louisiana CORE work (the Southern Consumers Cooperative case)
fills_historical_gaps.narrative_complications: Sobol's account significantly complicates the popular narrative of the Bogalusa Deacons for Defense by noting that he, working with the Voters League leadership in 1965-68, had little direct contact with a named 'Deacons' organization and that Lolis Elie shares his impression. This is a substantive historiographical contribution.
fills_historical_gaps.historiographical_value: Historians studying Title VII enforcement, the Lawyers Constitutional Defense Committee model, Louisiana federal-court civil rights litigation, the Bogalusa movement, or the lineage from McDaniels to Batson will find Sobol's testimony directly useful as primary-source material.
fills_historical_gaps.justification: Sobol's account fills several specific historiographical gaps -- the operational mechanics of LCDC, the procedural genealogy of the seniority-systems doctrine, and the on-the-ground perspective on the Bogalusa Voters League's relationship with the Deacons -- and provides verifiable case citations that anchor the testimony for cross-reference.

historical_value.overall_assessment: Historical value is high: Sobol's first-person account documents both nationally significant Title VII and criminal-procedure precedents and locally important Louisiana movement history, with the specificity and verifiability needed for scholarly use. Several elements (the LCDC permanent-staff model, the Sobol-Cooper article's lifecycle, the contrarian Deacons account) are particularly difficult to recover from other sources.

transformation_growth.transformation_arc: Sobol begins as a corporate associate at a prestigious Washington firm with 'illusions' about its civil rights pedigree and ends as a civil rights lawyer who finds it impossible to return to commercial practice; the transformation is presented as cumulative rather than sudden.
key_turning_points: First three-week LCDC volunteer assignment in August 1965 (Segment 41, 00:05:47) -- 'that letter changed my life'
Recognition during the volunteer summer that LCDC needed permanent rather than rotating lawyers (Segment 51-53, 00:08:08)
Decision at the end of his second leave-of-absence year that returning to Arnold, Fortas & Porter was 'completely out of the question' (Segment 1149-1150, 01:55:14)
transformation_growth.before_after_contrast: Before: Corporate associate handling antitrust litigation expecting a long firm career. After: Civil rights lawyer for unions in private practice with his own firm, who has argued before the U.S. Supreme Court and finds it inconceivable to represent 'one big corporation against another.'
transformation_growth.inspirational_quality: Sobol's account quietly models how a methodical professional's life can pivot decisively through extended exposure to a movement context; he extends this observation to the short-term volunteer lawyers and law students whose lives he says were similarly changed by 'contagion.'
transformation_growth.justification: The transformation arc is clearly articulated and supported by Sobol's own narrative of leaves-of-absence, return-decisions, and ultimately the impossibility of resuming his prior career. The interview frames this as professional and intellectual transformation more than as identity reformation, which keeps the score below 9.

peak_moments.description: Sobol's arrest by Leander Perez in the Plaquemines Parish courthouse hallway for practicing law without a license
peak_moments.stakes: Personal liability and potential exclusion of out-of-state civil rights lawyers from Louisiana practice

peak_moments.description: Bulldozers ready to clear Bogalusa public-housing land halted at the last minute by Judge Cassibry's order
peak_moments.stakes: Whether segregated housing siting could be enjoined under fair-housing law

peak_moments.description: The Bogalusa-to-Baton Rouge march through Livingston Parish (believed to be Klan headquarters), with National Guardsmen separating marchers and observers
Cross-examination of Crown Zellerbach expert witnesses with George Cooper's trunk of books that contradicted their prior writings
peak_moments.stakes: Outcome of an eight-day Title VII trial affecting hundreds of paper-mill workers

conflict_types: Legal-procedural conflict (judge-lawyer hostility, motions to dismiss, peremptory-challenge battles)
Institutional conflict (Sobol's office vs. Crown Zellerbach, vs. HUD, vs. Plaquemines Parish DA)
Personal physical-safety concern (Sobol's habit of never sleeping in Bogalusa, the Livingston Parish march)
tension_conflict_triumph.emotional_range: From measured legal-procedural narration to wry acknowledgment of personal risk (his arrest, his refusal to sleep in Bogalusa) to quiet pride in courtroom victories.
tension_conflict_triumph.justification: The interview contains several genuinely high-stakes moments -- the arrest, the bulldozer-stop, the march through Livingston Parish, the eight-day Crown Zellerbach trial -- but Sobol's narrative voice is consistently measured rather than dramatic, and several of the most dramatic events (Hicks v. Knight, the Klan violence) Sobol disclaims direct memory of. The score reflects solid dramatic material rendered in restrained professional tone.

universal_themes: Discovering one's vocation through unexpected exposure
The pull between security (the leave-of-absence safety net) and conviction
Working alongside committed local leaders who model perseverance
relatable_struggles: Choosing meaningful work over financial security
Risk and personal cost (the arrest, the union-establishment backlash) for principle
human_moments: Sobol's first-day arrival as a new permanent staff lawyer with four inches of case files he had never seen (Segment 159-163, 00:18:42)
The end-of-day scotch-and-conversation gatherings at Collins, Douglas, and Elie (Segment 91-94, 00:13:18)
Attending the Zippert wedding after winning the marriage-license case (Segment 1097-1098, 01:50:47)
relatable_experiences.connection_points: Sobol's professional-vocation story will resonate most directly with lawyers and law students; the human-interest moments (the wedding, the scotch sessions, the union-hall meetings) extend the reach somewhat but the interview is not principally framed around universal life moments.
relatable_experiences.justification: The interview's relatability is solid for legal-professional and movement-history audiences but is not broadly framed around universal life-moment themes (love, loss, family, religion) that would maximize this score. Sobol mentions his wife Anne and his children only briefly and largely in professional context.

emotional_resonance.overall_assessment: Emotional resonance is moderate. Sobol presents a clear and authentic transformation arc and recounts several high-stakes moments (the arrest, the Crown Zellerbach trial), but his narrative voice is consistently measured-professional rather than emotionally vulnerable. The interview's relatability skews toward legal-professional audiences.

barriers: Some legal jargon (interrogatories, peremptory challenges, lines of progression, summary reversal) used without immediate definition
Several proper nouns are not always immediately identified in context (Murphy Bell, Henry Schwarzschild, Carl Rachlin)
language_clarity.justification: Sobol is an articulate, fluent speaker with clear standard American English diction. He explains many technical concepts in passing -- particularly the Wonderlic test, the merged-pay-line seniority system, and the EEOC charge process -- but assumes some baseline familiarity with civil-procedure terminology that general audiences may need to look up.

high_energy_sections: The Wonderlic test and 'What is RSVP me?' anecdote (Segment 696-712, 00:57:58 - 01:01:22)
slow_sections: Detailed procedural discussion of LCDC volunteer-program funding economics (Segment 172-178, 00:19:27 - 00:19:54)
Extended technical explanation of how merged seniority lines worked (Segment 715-731, 01:02:11 - 01:06:01)
narrative_flow.attention_sustainability: A general audience may find the procedural-legal segments demanding; lawyers, civil rights historians, and oral-history researchers should find the entire interview engaging. The 1h55m length is substantial but not excessive given the breadth of cases covered.
narrative_flow.justification: The interview generally maintains momentum and Mosnier paces well with thematic prompts, but several legal-procedural segments slow the pace for general listeners. The eight-day trial discussion and the seniority-system technical explanation are particularly demanding.

themes_connected: Federal civil rights enforcement and the role of the federal judiciary
Translating Title VII / Civil Rights Act of 1964 from text to operational remedies
Cross-racial coalitional lawyering and the limits of out-of-state outsider activism
Personal transformation of professionals through movement work
Loving v. Virginia and interracial marriage rights as a concrete local-level practice issue
broader_themes_connection.contemporary_relevance: Sobol explicitly connects the legal craft of the 1960s to contemporary doctrinal regressions ('every judge on that district court and the court of appeals, they're all bad') and to ongoing debates about jury-selection (Batson) and disparate-impact theory in employment discrimination. His reflection that judicial outcomes are 'all how the judge feels' resonates with contemporary discussions of the federal bench.
broader_themes_connection.meaning_making: Sobol's reflective sections explicitly connect his case-by-case work to the larger arc of federal civil rights jurisprudence and to the lifelong career-arc question of how movement work changes its practitioners.
broader_themes_connection.justification: Sobol explicitly and repeatedly connects his Louisiana work to the larger arc of federal civil rights enforcement, to the role of the judiciary, to the personal cost and reward of professional transformation, and to specific contemporary issues (jury composition, peremptory challenges, judicial appointments). The connections are made plainly throughout the interview rather than reserved for a closing summary.

accessibility.overall_assessment: Accessibility is solid: Sobol is articulate, the interviewer scaffolds well, and Sobol explicitly connects his case-by-case work to larger civil rights themes and contemporary issues. The score is held below the maximum primarily because of the substantial procedural-legal content that will challenge general audiences.

engagement_scores.overall_assessment: This is a high-value, professionally narrated oral history that documents foundational Title VII, criminal-procedure, and fair-housing litigation in Louisiana from 1965 through 1974, anchored by a clear personal-transformation arc and a deliberately contrarian perspective on Bogalusa movement historiography. Sobol's narrative voice is measured and analytical rather than emotionally raw, which makes the interview a particularly strong resource for legal historians and movement-history scholars even as it limits its relatability for general audiences. The breadth of cases covered, the concreteness of names and citations, and the explicit historiographical interventions (especially on the Deacons for Defense) place this in the top tier of Civil Rights History Project oral histories for primary-source research utility.
key_strengths: Exceptional specificity in case citations, judicial names, and procedural detail -- a primary-source-quality account of the federal civil rights litigation infrastructure in 1960s Louisiana

Clearly articulated personal transformation arc tied to the Lawyers Constitutional Defense Committee's permanent-staff model, with direct extension to the broader phenomenon of how short-term volunteer lawyers were changed by movement work
Substantive historiographical intervention on the Bogalusa Deacons for Defense and Justice (Sobol's skeptical perspective is offered explicitly as a counterweight to dominant narrative)
key_limitations: Measured-professional narrative voice limits emotional vulnerability and reduces relatability for general audiences

Several memory-based date and citation references are explicitly hedged by Sobol himself ('1976, maybe 75'), and at least one case-name (the Quarles trial judge) is mis-rendered in the transcript -- creating verification work for downstream historians

claims.claim: Sobol was born in New York City and lived on the west side of Manhattan.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I grew up in New York City. I lived on the west side of Manhattan. My father was a lawyer. My mother was a mathematics teacher (Segments 11-12)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement.

claims.claim: Sobol attended the Bronx High School of Science, Union College, and Columbia Law School.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I went to the Bronx High School of Science, and I went to college, a union college, and was connected in New York, and came back to New York to go to Columbia Law School (Segments 13-14)
claims.rationale: Direct statement; 'a union college' is rendered slightly ambiguously but the canonical reading is Union College in Schenectady, New York.

claims.claim: Sobol clerked for Judge Paul R. Hays on the United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I went from law school to being a law clerk for Judge Paul R. Hays on the United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit (Segment 21)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement.

claims.claim: Sobol joined Arnold, Fortas & Porter in spring 1963 as an associate.
claims.supporting_excerpt: in the spring of 1963 I went to work for, was then a Arnold, Fortas & Porter as an associate (Segment 23)
claims.rationale: Direct statement.

claims.claim: The Lawyers Constitutional Defense Committee was directed by Henry Schwarzschild.
claims.supporting_excerpt: during the course of the following winter, I met the director of the program, Henry Schwarzschild (Segment 37)
claims.rationale: Direct statement naming Schwarzschild as the program director.

claims.claim: Sobol arrived in New Orleans on August 1, 1965, for a three-week LCDC assignment.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I got here the first of August 1965, and I spent my three weeks (Segment 41)
claims.rationale: Direct statement.

claims.claim: On Sobol's first day he was directed to meet Murphy Bell in Baton Rouge, who described a school desegregation case in St. Francisville (West Feliciana Parish).
claims.supporting_excerpt: drive up to Baton Rouge and meet Murphy Bell. He has a school case he wants to bring... the people in St. Francisville, West Feliciana Parish, want to bring a school desigregation case (Segment 44)
claims.rationale: Direct statement; spelling 'desigregation' is Whisper transcription error for 'desegregation,' canonical correction.

claims.claim: Judge E. Gordon West, sitting in Baton Rouge, dismissed the school desegregation case the day after filing; the Fifth Circuit reversed within roughly a week.
claims.supporting_excerpt: The judge there was a well-known racist judge named E. Gordon West, and he would just throw all these cases out... And the day after I filed it, the case was dismissed, and I filed a motion in the Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals for a summary reversal of Judge West's decision. And a week later, the Fifth Circuit reversed the decision (Segments 47-49)
claims.rationale: Direct sequential statement of events.

claims.claim: Sobol associated with Collins, Douglas, and Elie -- the law firm of Robert F. Collins, Nils Douglas, and Lolis Elie -- whose office was at 2200 Dryad Street.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was working with the law firm, Collins, Douglas, and Elie, which is a very important, the most important firm in civil rights law in Louisiana, three young black lawyers... Robert F. Collins, who later became a federal judge, the first black federal judge in the South. Nils Douglas... And Lolis Elie (Segments 54, 83-85). 'the office of Collins, Douglas, and Eli was on the second floor in the 2200 block of Dryad Street' (Segment 66)
claims.rationale: Direct statements identifying the three partners and the office location.

claims.claim: Robert F. Collins later became the first Black federal judge in the South.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Robert F. Collins, who later became a federal judge, the first black federal judge in the South (Segment 83)
claims.rationale: The transcript supports Sobol's claim verbatim. Historical record commonly describes Collins as the first Black federal judge in Louisiana (appointed 1978); the broader 'in the South' characterization is the witness's framing and may overstate (e.g., earlier appointments in other Southern states could complicate). Marked partially_supported because the transcript supports the witness's words but the underlying historical claim is potentially imprecise.

claims.claim: The Bogalusa Voters League leadership comprised A.Z. Young as president, Robert (Bob) Hicks as vice president, and Gayle Jenkins as secretary.
claims.supporting_excerpt: A.Z. Young was the president. And Bob was the vice president, and Gail was the secretary, and never changed (Segments 408-411)
claims.rationale: Direct statement of organizational roles.

claims.claim: Crown Zellerbach was the major employer in Bogalusa, with four plants making paper products, boxes, and wrapping paper.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Crown Zellerbach, which was the major employer in town had four plants of paper products, boxes, wrapping paper, different things (Segments 113-115)
claims.rationale: Direct statement.

claims.claim: Title VII of the Civil Rights Act took effect approximately four weeks before Sobol's arrival in Bogalusa in August 1965.
claims.supporting_excerpt: The Title VII of the Civil Rights Act had just come into effect four weeks earlier (Segment 112)
claims.rationale: Direct statement; Title VII took effect July 2, 1965, consistent with the four-weeks-before-August-1 timing.

claims.claim: The Crown Zellerbach Title VII trial was tried before Judge Frederick J.R. Heebe, consolidated with United States v. Local 1.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Another was there was a new judge just appointed named Judge Frederick J.R. Heebe... it came to trial, was consolidated with a government case, United States versus local one (Segments 474, 444-445)
claims.rationale: Direct statement.

claims.claim: Sobol co-authored the Harvard Law Review article on seniority systems with George Cooper.
claims.supporting_excerpt: George and I were all friends... we sent it to the Harvard Law Review. They edited the whole thing... it was in the Harvard Law Review (Segments 757, 763-765)
claims.rationale: Direct statement.

claims.claim: The Supreme Court ruled against the Sobol-Cooper seniority theory in United States v. Teamsters in 1975 or 1976.
claims.supporting_excerpt: in a case involving, I think, the United States versus Teamsters in 1976, maybe 75 (Segment 769)
claims.rationale: Sobol's own statement is explicitly hedged. The decision commonly cited is Teamsters v. United States, 431 U.S. 324 (1977). Marked partially_supported because the transcript itself frames the date as a memory approximation.

claims.claim: Gary Duncan was 19 years old when he came to Sobol's office in early 1967.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Gary Duncan and his mother... Gary was 19 years old at the time. His mother came to my office (Segment 812)
claims.rationale: Direct statement.

claims.claim: Leander Perez was a 'well-known racist figure' whose son was the district attorney in Plaquemines Parish, and Perez appointed the judges.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Leander Perez was a well-known racist figure who espoused theories of interposition and secession... His son was a district attorney and he appointed the judges (Segments 816, 819)
claims.rationale: Direct statement.

claims.claim: Sobol was arrested by Leander Perez and a sheriff in the Plaquemines Parish courthouse on charges of practicing law without a license; Don Juneau bailed him out the same day.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Leander Perez and a sheriff standing there in the hall arrested me. And the charge was practicing law without a license... Don Juneau came down and bailed me out before the afternoon was out (Segments 837-839)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement.

claims.claim: Duncan v. Louisiana resulted in a Supreme Court ruling that the Fourteenth Amendment requires jury trials in state criminal proceedings except for the most minor offenses.
claims.supporting_excerpt: decision came down ruling that it's illegal to file a violates the 14th amendment not to allow juries and state trials except for the most minor crimes (Segment 860)
claims.rationale: Direct statement, consistent with the canonical 1968 ruling in Duncan v. Louisiana, 391 U.S. 145.

claims.claim: The federal three-judge court included Judges Cassibry, Heebe, and Robert A. Ainsworth Jr.
claims.supporting_excerpt: the case was assigned to Judge Fred J. Cassibry... A three-judge court was assembled, which included Judge Frederick J.R. Heebe, my friend. And Judge Robert A. Ainsworth Jr, a member of the Court of Appeals (Segment 843)
claims.rationale: Direct statement, including the spelling of Cassibry's first name and middle initial.

claims.claim: Zelma Wyche, head of the Madison Parish voters league, lost a police-chief election by 10-15 votes after a clerk of court collected approximately 200 absentee ballots from white nursing homes.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Zelma Wyche who was the head of the Madison parish voters league who was running for police chief... he lost the election when Frisk was came by by about 10-15 votes... the clerk of court had gone to white nursing homes... And he had collected something like 200 ballots that way (Segments 874-878)
claims.rationale: Direct statement of facts and numbers.

claims.claim: Harrison Brown, a Black teacher running for a Madison Parish school board seat, was disconnected from the Democratic Party 'rooster' lever; the judge ordered a new election and Brown won.
claims.supporting_excerpt: there was a black candidate, a man named Harrison Brown, who was running for the school board. He was a teacher in the school system. And they disconnected that position from the line... the judge found that there's another instance of discrimination... he required that election to be held again. And it was held again. And Harrison Brown won (Segments 900-916)
claims.rationale: Direct statement of facts.

claims.claim: Hicks v. Weaver, 302 F. Supp. 619, was a federal fair-housing case before Judge Cassibry concerning the racially discriminatory siting of public housing in Bogalusa.
claims.supporting_excerpt: The case of Hicks v. Weaver, 302 F. Supp. 619 (Segment 951)... Judge Casabray found we did have a right of action and that we, it was discriminatory to do that and he enjoined the construction of the housing (Segment 939)
claims.rationale: Direct citation by witness; spelling of judge's name varies in transcript (Casabray) but refers to Cassibry.

claims.claim: Judge Alvin B. Rubin's ruling in United States v. McDaniels found that peremptory challenges could not be used to remove every Black juror; this anticipated the Supreme Court's later Batson v. Kentucky, 476 U.S. 79, ruling.
claims.supporting_excerpt: we convinced Judge Alvin B. Rubin, the trial judge, that he could be any reason they want except race... that's the reported decision called United States v. McDaniels... 20 years later, Supreme Court adopted that as a rule of national applicability in Batson v. Kentucky, 476 U.S. 79 (Segments 997-1001)
claims.rationale: Direct statement.

claims.claim: After leaving Louisiana, Sobol taught one semester at the University of Michigan Law School (January 1969) and then joined Marian Wright Edelman's Washington Research Project (predecessor to the Children's Defense Fund).
claims.supporting_excerpt: I had been offered a position teaching in the University of Michigan Law School. And in, at the first of the year, moved out to Michigan. I did, I didn't like January 69... after one semester, I left and I went to work for Marian Wright Edelman, Marian Wright Edelman at the, what was then called the Washington Research Project (Segments 1014-1028)
claims.rationale: Direct statement; the Washington Research Project was indeed Edelman's organization, later rechristened the Children's Defense Fund in 1973.

claims.claim: The Southern Consumers Cooperative, organized with CORE worker John Zippert and Catholic priest Father Albert J. McKnight, processed and sold sweet potatoes near Opelousas and Lafayette and was the subject of federal litigation after the local district attorney harassed the cooperative.
claims.supporting_excerpt: John Zippert, and a Catholic priest who had been assigned to a parish in that district name of Father Albert J. McKnight... And many of the local farmers formed a, a farm's cooperative to process package and sell the sweet potatoes... we sued in federal court in Lafayette... the organization is still there. It's called the Southern Consumers Cooperative (Segments 1062-1076)
claims.rationale: Direct statement; the cooperative is named imprecisely by witness ('Southern Consumers Cooperative cooperative... it's got the other word') so the canonical name may differ slightly.

claims.claim: After Loving v. Virginia, Sobol obtained a federal order requiring the Lafayette-area clerk of court to issue an interracial marriage license to John Zippert and Carol Zippert.
claims.supporting_excerpt: the Supreme Court had just decided loving against Virginia. I walled in there with my suit to join me to require the clerk of court to give him a marriage license. And of course, he did. I got to go to the wedding (Segments 1093-1097)
claims.rationale: Direct statement; consistent with the ground-truth corpus entry for Loving v. Virginia (1967).

claims.claim: Anne Sobol, Sobol's then-wife, worked with Lolis Elie and Sobol on the welfare-fraud defense cases in New Orleans federal court.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Anne Sobol and I, my wife Anne Sobol and I and Lola C. Light became involved in representing a group of women who were being criminally prosecuted in federal court New Orleans (Segment 960)
claims.rationale: Direct statement; 'Lola C. Light' is a Whisper rendering error for 'Lolis Elie' (the partner of Collins, Douglas, and Elie repeatedly identified throughout the interview); correction applied per the corrected-transcript convention.

claims.claim: Sobol's later D.C. private firm was opened with Mike Trister and represented unions.
claims.supporting_excerpt: one of the other people in that office, Mike Trister, I opened a law firm on our own. And practice together (Segments 1049-1050)
claims.rationale: Direct statement.

publication_decision.rationale: The main summary's accuracy score (88) is below the 90 publication threshold, primarily because of memory-hedged dates and citations that Sobol himself flagged as approximate (e.g., the year of the Supreme Court's Teamsters ruling) and because the interview date is missing. The citation audit found 28 of 30 main-summary claims supported and 2 partially supported (Collins as 'first Black federal judge in the South' -- transcript-supported witness framing but historically debatable; Teamsters case dated 1975/76 -- witness-hedged but actually 1977). No claim was unsupported. Under the fail-closed gate, even partial-support claims and a sub-90 accuracy score route the summary to human review, which is the correct outcome. A reviewer should confirm the interview date, verify Hicks v. Weaver and Teamsters citations, and decide whether Sobol's 'first Black federal judge in the South' framing needs hedging in the published summary.
